title: Garlic and thyme oxymel
subtitle: A honey-vinegar respiratory syrup
excerpt: Garlic and thyme soaked in raw honey and apple cider vinegar. An oxymel (honey-vinegar preparation) traditionally used at the onset of a cold for its antimicrobial and expectorant action.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, garlic entry: the cold and respiratory infection tradition, the honey preparation. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Allium sativum: the oxymel and syrup forms for coughs and colds. The oxymel form (mel + acetum) appears in Hippocratic writings and throughout the European herbal herbal pharmacopoeia tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow to soaked 1-2 days before using; the longer soaking draws more of the allicin and thymol into the honey-vinegar medium. Keeps 3 months in the fridge.

projectSchedule.title: Assemble and cover
projectSchedule.body: Combine the garlic and thyme in a jar, pour over the honey and vinegar, and seal. The soaking begins immediately.

projectSchedule.title: Strain and bottle
projectSchedule.body: After 2 days, strain out the garlic and thyme and pour the finished oxymel into a clean jar. It is ready to use.

glossaryTerms.definition: A traditional preparation combining honey (mel) and vinegar (acetum) as a medium for herb maceration. Oxymel forms appear in Hippocratic medicine and throughout the European pharmacopoeia. They extract a different range of compounds than either medium alone.

The sulphur compound responsible for garlic's distinctive smell and traditional antimicrobial reputation. It forms when garlic cells are crushed, activating the enzyme alliinase. It is unstable; freshly crushed garlic has the most.

content.text: is one of the oldest forms in the European herbal tradition: herbs soaked in equal parts honey and apple cider vinegar. This garlic and thyme version is a straightforward cold-season preparation. The garlic provides the

-derived antimicrobial action; the thyme adds its own thymol and phlegm-loosening aromatic oil. The honey and vinegar medium preserves the preparation for 3 months in the fridge. The result tastes sharp, garlicky, and sweet all at once. A teaspoon at the onset of a cold, or up to three times a day during one, is the traditional dose.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Anticoagulants. Garlic has well-documented antiplatelet activity and can increase bleeding risk in readers on warfarin, aspirin at therapeutic dose, or similar anticoagulants. Do not use this preparation if you are on anticoagulant medication without checking with your prescriber.

Honey and infants. Do not give honey-containing preparations to children under one year (infant botulism risk).

Pregnancy. Culinary garlic in food is safe throughout pregnancy. Regular therapeutic use of garlic is outside the culinary safe-use context. Check with your midwife before regular use in pregnancy.

items.prepNote: peeled and crushed or roughly chopped; crushing activates the allicin
fresh thyme sprigs; dried works too (use 5 g)
raw honey if available; any runny honey works
raw apple cider vinegar; with-the-mother if available

content.text: Crush or roughly chop the garlic cloves. Layer the garlic and thyme into a 200 ml glass jar. Combine the honey and apple cider vinegar and pour over to cover the herbs. The honey will dissolve into the vinegar as the jar warms; stir once after an hour if the honey is sitting at the bottom. Seal the jar and label it with the date. Leave to soaked for 1-2 days at room temperature, shaking gently once or twice a day.

After 2 days, strain through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing the garlic and thyme to extract all the liquid. Discard the spent herbs. Pour the finished oxymel into a clean jar, seal, and refrigerate. It should smell strongly of garlic and thyme, taste sweet-sharp, and be amber to golden in colour.

Adults: 1 teaspoon (5 ml) at the onset of a cold, then up to three times a day during the acute episode. Take neat or stirred into a little warm water. Do not exceed 3 teaspoons per day. Use for the duration of an acute cold, typically 5-7 days. Children over one year: half a teaspoon, up to twice a day. Paediatric use under 12: consult a herbalist.
Sealed in a glass jar in the fridge, the oxymel keeps 3 months. Discard if it smells fermented, develops mould, or tastes noticeably off.
When not to use this remedy

On any anticoagulant medication. In infants under one year (honey risk). Respiratory symptoms with significant breathlessness, high fever lasting more than 3 days, pain on swallowing, or cough with blood all need medical assessment.
